topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery notification blinked on my phone, a mundane reminder of a package arriving. I walked to the door, my heart giving an odd, tight flutter against my ribs. It was addressed to Apartment 3B, the unit I had just vacated last month. I took the box, the cardboard surprisingly heavy, and carried it into the hallway. The handwriting on the label was familiar, a scrawl I vaguely recognized from the previous lease agreement. As I set it down on my worn welcome mat, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. Inside, nestled in packing peanuts, was a collection of antique porcelain dolls, some with unsettlingly vacant eyes. I stared at them, my breath catching in my throat, unable to move or process what I was seeing.

[story 2]

I found the package sitting by the mail slot, a stark contrast to the usual junk mail. It was clearly marked with a previous tenant’s name. My fingers trembled slightly as I peeled back the packing tape. The contents spilled out—a stack of old, brittle photographs, faded letters tied with fraying ribbon, and a small, tarnished silver locket. A sudden wave of disorientation washed over me. My eyes darted from one item to the next, my mouth slightly agape. I reread the address on the exterior, then looked back at the items, a strange, breathless silence filling the small entryway. I backed away slowly, my gaze fixed on the contents as if they might suddenly move.

[story 3]

When the courier handed me the parcel, I felt an immediate, visceral alarm. It was clearly marked for the former resident of this very apartment. I took it with a stiffness that bordered on rigidity. The moment I opened the box, the air seemed to leave my lungs. It contained an entire collection of handwritten journals, bound in worn leather. My hands, which had been steady moments before, began to shake uncontrollably. I dropped the box onto the floor, the sound echoing loudly in the quiet apartment. I knelt down, my vision blurring slightly as I tried to make sense of the intimate, private history contained within those pages.
